design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore locations, gather inventory items, solve context-sensitive puzzles by combining items or performing actions on NPCs and objects; progress through chapters by completing objective chains
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Rescue Elaine from curse/LeChuck while solving whimsical, character-driven puzzles across exotic pirate locations
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a clever pirate adventurer who talks, thinks, and problem-solves through absurdist humor and lateral thinking rather than combat
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Prioritizes witty dialogue, character personality, and narrative humor over mechanical challenge; refuses combat-based progression in favor of insult sword-fighting and puzzle-solving; expects players to accept inventory-based logic puzzles as the primary gameplay verb

difficulty.checkpointing: Chapter-based progression with ability to revisit locations; game does not appear to have fail states that produce game-over conditions
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player may select between normal mode and 'Mega-Monkey' mode with additional puzzles at game start; during chapter 3, player can choose between assisted ship combat or full manual control of sea battles

progression.upgrade_system: Ship cannons can be upgraded through sea battle treasure acquisition; inventory items are combined to create new tools (e.g., diamond + engagement band = diamond ring); crew assembly and pirate insult vocabulary expansion
progression.unlock_mechanism: Chapter-gated progression; objectives unlock new locations and NPC interactions; obtaining specific items (maps, ship, crew members) gates access to Blood Island and later areas
replayability_hooks: Mega-Monkey mode provides alternative puzzle solutions for experienced players
Ship combat manual vs. assisted choice in chapter 3 creates branching mechanical experience
Hidden dialogue and character interactions reward thorough exploration
Insult sword-fighting sequences allow for multiple valid player-generated responses

world_building_techniques: Fully voiced character dialogue with distinctive personalities for main cast
Animated cutscenes showcasing plot developments and character reactions
Environmental storytelling through location design (e.g., chicken restaurant, barber shops, theater, exclusive beach club)
NPC-driven exposition via conversation trees and character interactions
Absurdist humor and pop-culture references woven into puzzle design and dialogue
Chapter structure provides narrative pacing and story beats

boss_design.philosophy: Non-combat-focused confrontations resolved through talking and wit rather than reflexes; bosses are social/intellectual challenges dressed in pirate adventure framing
notable_bosses: LeChuck (recurring antagonist, battles through puzzle-solving rather than combat)
Captain Rottingham (defeated via rhyming insult sword-fighting)
Insult sword-fighting sequences (dialogue-based duels with pattern-matching elements)

faction_indicators: Pirate crew assembly (three barber pirates join Guybrush)
Ship upgrade economics (sea battle treasure determines firepower and status)
Insult sword-fighting mastery as social dominance signal
danger_signals: LeChuck's transformation into fiery demon represents escalating threat level
Storm-forced shipwreck on Blood Island signals entry into dangerous territory
Voodoo curse mechanics establish supernatural threat persistence
environmental_storytelling: Plunder Island siege depicts LeChuck's threat to established settlement
Cannibals transformed into vegetarians shows world changes between games
Stan's insurance business on Blood Island reveals post-adventure economic reality
Ship mechanics and sea battle gameplay reflect pirate commerce and naval hierarchy

sources_used: wikipedia: The Curse of Monkey Island - Gameplay section, Plot section, general infobox data
